content:
To those of you who are interested in categorical probability, categorical quantum mechanics, and/or potential applications of these to information processing, cryptography or causal inference, I'd like to advertise the recent thesis of Nicholas Gauguin Houghton-Larsen at the University of Copenhagen. It's here:

https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.02302

This document is a treasure trove of ideas and results that may be of interest for years to come, and it's quite a pleasant read on top of that. Unfortunately, Nicholas may be about to leave the scientific community, and it is unclear whether any of this will appear in any other form than in the thesis. I'm therefore trying to advertise this material a bit in order to prevent it from going unnoticed, especially as Nicholas has not known about the ACT community until very recently.

As a teaser, here's a selection-biased summary of the things that I've personally found the most interesting:

  • Chapter 2 shows that one can already do a surprising amount of categorical probability and categorical quantum mechanics in any semicartesian monoidal category satisfying a bunch of natural axioms. These axioms are then used to prove an entirely new generalization of the no-cloning theorem as Theorem 2.5.18, which in addition also specializes to the recent no-masking theorem and other results of this type. I'm already using some of the chapter's theory of dilations in my own work.
  • Chapter 3 considers the extension to categories enriched in metric spaces and proposes axioms for how the metric ought to be compatible with dilations. It applies these definitions and results in order to develop a new notion of distance of quantum channels.
  • Chapter 4 is the heart of the thesis. It develops a categorical framework for the joint analysis of causal structure and information leaking to and from the environment. Among other things, this involves a generalization of traced monoidal categories that seems to be new. It clearly provides a convincing categorical setting for causal inference. I also wonder if this framework could serve as a foundation for a categorical approach to cryptography, at least insofar as the analysis of information flow and information leakage is concerned.

So if you know of any others who may be interested in this sort of thing but may not read this forum, please spread the word!
By chapter 2, do you mean that it's possible to something similar to Markov categories but without the comultiplication/copy morphisms?

I just mean that it's been surprising to me personally that one can do anything that vaguely resembles probability theory or a nontrivial theory of information flow with semicartesianness only. I can't say for sure that one can do as much with it as one can do with Markov categories, but I believe that the answer is yes, in the following (uninteresting) way.
My hypothesis is that suitable versions of Nicholas's axioms, in particular universal dilations, will imply that copy morphisms exist, with the idea being that copying and leaking one copy is the universal way of leaking information to the enviroment. If this conjecture is true, then being a Markov category is morally an extra property of a semicartesian category rather than extra structure. (Although it technically is extra structure, that extra structure is unique in typical examples and behaves more like extra property.) Does this sound plausible?
